feed.title: SAP Community - Cloud Integration

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation
entry.content:
Authors: Adam Csallokozi, David Domokos

  • Let’s first determine your environment
  • Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount
  • 1. Create the CF Subaccount
  • 2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount
  • 3. Enable Cloud Foundry
  • Verify the Destination Service
  • 1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys
  • 2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)
  • 3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service
  • 4. Generate the Certificate viaDestination Service REST API
  • 5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate
  • Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)
  • Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP
  • Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:
  • Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:
  • Conclusion

Driven by an industry-wide change by Certification Authorities (see SAP KBA 3711618 – Client Authentication EKU Deprecation), newly procured publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates may omit the “Client Authentication” Extended Key Usage (EKU) starting May 2026 as CAs separate server and client roles. For inbound scenarios that use Client Certificate Authentication (two‑way SSL/mTLS) with SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration, or API Management, the client certificate must include the Client Authentication EKU; certificates lacking this EKU will not be accepted by the Integration Suite endpoints.

There are multiple ways to implement this requirement. In this article,  we will demonstrate one of the three supported approaches, including the technical steps needed to generate and use the client certificate in your integration scenarios.

Let’s first determine your environment 

Before starting the implementation, first determine which environment applies to your landscape. The steps in this guide differ depending on the environment used for generating and signing the client certificate:

  • If you are working on Cloud Foundry environment please start with Verify the Destination Service chapter

  • If you are working on Neo environment please start with the Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount chapter

 

Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount

ℹ️ Please note that all steps described in this section can be performed free of charge and do not involve any additional costs ℹ️

⚠️ Please be aware to perform the listed actions you need to have a Global Account Admin role or appropriate admin colleague is required⚠️

If you are working on Neo environment, first create a new, Cloud Foundry (CF) Subaccount. This is required to use the Destination service to sign certificate.

1. Create the CF Subaccount

Go to your Global Account and click on 'Create' then choose 'Subaccount'

2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount

 Please fill the required fields:

  • Display Name: Choose the desired name for your subaccount
  • Region: Choose the desired region for your subaccount

the rest of the fields will be filled automatically, after that click on 'Create'.

3. Enable Cloud Foundry

 

Once the Subaccount has been created, please open it. Click on “Enable Cloud Foundry” button. In the "Enable Cloud Foundry" popup window, input desired value, then press button "Create". Once see "Cloud Foundry Environment" section get populated with Cloud Foundry organization data, the Subaccount creation is successfully completed. 

ℹ️If you need more detailed guide to complete this step you can check our Help Page: Create a Subaccount ℹ️

Verify the Destination Service

In case you are running Cloud Integration / Integration Suite with CF environment, then please go to the CF subaccount where Cloud Integration / Integration Suite is subscribed. In case you are running Cloud Integration with NEO environment, then please go to the newly created CF subaccount as mentioned in chapter 'Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount'.  Then access  menu 'Entitlements' and confirm 'Destination Service' with service plan 'lite' is listed.

 

1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys

In BTP Cockpit, go to ‘Instances and Subscriptions’ on the left, then choose the ‘Create’ button in the top right corner

In the pop-up, set Service to Destination Service and Plan to lite. Select the Space where you have administrator permissions, then enter an Instance Name that follows your naming convention. Once the Create button is enabled, click Create.

⚠️When you enable Cloud Foundry (CF) for a new subaccount, only the org is created by default, no spaces are created. As a result, when you create a Destination Service instance, you may see a popup (as shown below) prompting you to create a space first ⚠️

  The instance is now created and visible on the Instances list.

Next, create a service key for the instance. Click the “lite” link to open the details panel on the right, navigate to the Service Keys section, and choose Create.

 

In the pop-up, enter a name for the Service Key, then click Create.

You should now see the newly created service key listed:

Finally, click View Credentials to display the generated credentials; you’ll use them in the next chapter.


 

2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)

⚠️Command line (CMD) should be opened directly in the folder where the .KEY and .CSR files should be saved + OpenSSL needs to be installed on the computer ⚠️

To generate the private key and the CSR signed by a newly generated private key use openSSL. Execute the following command:

openssl req -utf8 -nodes -sha256 -newkey rsa:3072 -keyout genExample.key -out genExample.csr

⚠️All CSR attributes (shown below) will be overwritten during generation, so avoid spending too much time configuring them ⚠️

The command generates genExample.key (private key). You won’t need it for the API steps, but keep it for later use (for example, when creating a .PFX). As a last step of this chapter, the generated .CSR file needs to be Base64‑encoded. This can be done (in case of Windows operating system) via Windows Powershell.

Open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing genExample.csr and run the command:

[Convert]::ToBase64String([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("genExample.csr"))

as a result the Base64 encoded value of the .CSR file will be displayed in the PowerShell window:

Please copy that value and save it because it will be needed in a later step, you can save it as simple text file in the same folder as the previously created files.

3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service

In this step the OAuth token will be generated that is needed for the certificate creation. To retrieve that OAuth token, either follow the API documentation, or download our predefined REST API collection from here. This collection will be used for the rest if the steps in the 4th and 5th chapters.

Open the “DestinationServiceTokenGeneration” request and populate its variables with the actual values from the credentials you created in the last step of Chapter 1 (Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys).

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)url (https://XXXX.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com)
clientID (in the Body section)clientid (sb-clone9ae33f841f774443XXXXXXXXXXXXX)
clientSecret (in the Body section)clientsecret (1c8d712b-b4ed-42ff-bb17-c2c8db2e166b$ROCeUoa1GXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

After you send the request with your actual values, the response body will include the OAuth token:

 

4. Generate the Certificate via Destination Service REST API

From the collection, open the request 'DestinationServiceCertificateGenerator'

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
Name (in payload of the body)Desired name of the created certificate (TestCert)
CN_value (in payload of the body)Common Name, represents the primary, specific domain name (MyDomain)
Base64_Encoded_CSR (in payload of the body)Paste the Base64 encoded value of .CSR that is created as a last step in the chapter '2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)'
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

In the response of the API call the HTTP 201 - Created should be appear as response code:

 

5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate

From the collection, open the request 'FetchNewCertificate' and a last step retrieve the created certificate chain.

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
name (in the URL section)In the previous step created name of the certificate (TestCert)
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

The API should return HTTP 200 OK, and the payload contains the newly created certificate chain, this can be found in the 'Content' field:

Save this value in a text file (base64.txt), because it is needed to Base64-decode the value to get the certificate chain. To perform the Base64 decoding open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing the recently saved file and run the command:

$base64 = Get-Content base64.txt -Raw
[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes("certChain.txt", [Convert]::FromBase64String($base64))

This command will:

  • read the Base64 string from base64.txt
  • Convert it to bytes
  • Write the bytes to certChain.txt in the folder where the PowerShell was opened

Once the command completes, verify that the created file contains content like:

You can then save it as a .PEM file and generate a .PFX for client certificate based authentication, by referring to the blog “Client Certificate Authentication in SAP Cloud Integration: Configuration and Best Practices,” section 1.1/A, “Generating the Client Certificate (.PFX file) – Using OpenSSL.”

ℹ️BTP Connectivity Service on CN data centers signs the keypair using [CDC Cloud Root CA] ℹ️

Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)

This section explains how to create and load a client certificate in S/4HANA using transaction STRUST. If you’re connecting from S/4HANA, you can generate the CSR directly in STRUST - no OpenSSL needed. After generating the CSR, use the Destination Service API to sign it and obtain the client certificate, then import the signed certificate back into the client PSE in STRUST for client certificate–based authentication.

 

  1. Create an SSL client PSE in S/4HANA; for exact steps refer to 2. Configuration Steps in the Backend 

  2. Right‑click the PSE and choose Create. (Make sure you are in Change mode.)

  3. Generate new Client Certificate and sign the certificate: STRUST -> Edit -> Create certificate request.

  4. In the popup, select Use PSE algorithm as the Signature algorithm, and press Continue.

  5. Save the signing request, which should have the following format:
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----
    <CSR>
    -----END CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----

  6. Encode the CSR in Base64 (the Destination Service API expects a Base64 string without the PEM headers/footers).

  7. In the DestinationServiceCertificateGeneration request, copy the encoded CSR in the body in the parameter "CSR".

  8. After the certificate is signed by the Destination Service API, Base64‑decode it, then in STRUST choose Edit -> Import certificate response. Import the full certificate chain (from the decoded file).

Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP

Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:

In Neo, inbound client-certificate authentication is configured by uploading the caller’s public certificate and mapping it to a platform user. This is done in the Certificate-to-User Mapping monitor in the Operations view.

  1. Open your Cloud Integration tenant and switch to Operations.
  2. Go to Manage Security > Certificate-to-User Mapping.
  3. Click Add and upload the client’s public certificate file (.cer). Upload only the leaf certificate of the client, not the chain.
  4. Assign a user name to this certificate. Use the technical platform user that should run the iFlow.
  5. Save the mapping.

Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:

To make the client certificate available to your Integration flows, create an external-certificate service key:

  1. Go to the SAP BTP cockpit, open your subaccount, and navigate to Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Select your desired 'Process Integration Runtime' with plan 'integration-flow' service instance
  3. Choose Create under Service Keys
  4. Enter a name for the service key
  5. Set Key Type to External Certificate and paste the PEM-encoded client certificate (only the first/leaf certificate, not the chain) into the External Certificate field
  6. Select Create

For more detailed steps, refer to the KBA 3297437  - Client Cert Authentication for Inbound calls to CPI tenants on CF.

Conclusion

The upcoming EKU deprecation underscores the need to proactively adjust certificate management practices in SAP Integration Suite landscapes. By implementing the recommended steps, organizations can maintain secure, compliant client certificate authentication for inbound scenarios. Preparing early helps prevent disruptions and ensures integrations continue to run smoothly as industry standards evolve.

entry.title: Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of version 1.0.4 of the Cloud Integration Pipeline package. This update introduces several key innovations, including the support for Virtual Backend Landscape Stages managed within a single Pipeline package and a single Integration Suite tenant.

Introduction & Motivation

This feature evolves the landscape support first introduced in version 1.0.9 of our legacy (now deprecated) all-in-one Process Integration Pipeline package. Previously, the pipeline allowed you to run scenarios across different stages without altering Partner Directory configurations - such as XSLTs for receiver determination. It achieved this by mapping the actual Sender Business System name to a "Sender Alias" based on the tenant's runtime environment.

The Challenge: The 1:1 Limitation

While the previous model worked seamlessly for 1:1 landscapes (e.g., one DEV tenant for one DEV backend), it struggled with multiple backend landscapes in one tenant. A common challenge arises when a single non-production SAP Integration Suite tenant is used to integrate both Development and Quality Assurance backends (e.g., S/4HANA).

In such cases, the landscape stage was "locked" to the tenant. You could define the stage for S/4HANA DEV or S/4HANA QA, but not both simultaneously. This forced integrators into a workaround: one system would have to be defined with its actual technical name (e.g., S4DCLNT100) in the Partner Directory, breaking the clean abstraction that the pipeline aims to provide.

The Solution: Virtual Stages

The new Virtual Stages feature is designed specifically for non-productive tenants where a single integration scenario must serve multiple landscape stages of the same backend.

Historically, achieving this in SAP Process Orchestration (PO) was also cumbersome. Typical approaches:

  • Backend Inbound Scenarios: Creating "dummy" XPath conditions in Receiver Determinations to switch between receivers.
  • Backend Outbound Scenarios: Duplicating Integrated Configurations (ICOs) for every actual sender.

The Cloud Integration Pipeline now provides an elegant, single-package solution for these requirements. In the next increments it is planned to add an option to define Integration Scenario in the context of a stage (e.g. if Scenario changed in Development stage but still old version is used in Quality).

Core Use Cases

We have optimized this framework to cover three primary architectural scenarios:

  1. Consolidated Non-Prod Environments: Supporting a 3-tier backend landscape using a 2-tier Integration Suite setup. This allows you to manage multiple backend stages through one pipeline in a single tenant.
  2. Dual-Track Backend Support: SAP S/4HANA landscapes often utilize "Maintenance" and "Project" tracks. Maintaining a dual-track middleware landscape to match this is a significant overhead. Virtual Stages allow you to support track-based routing for the backend using a single middleware instance.
  3. Cross-Stage Multicasting: More exotic use case - you may need to route a single message from a sender to multiple stages of the same receiver (e.g., sending data to both MDG-DEV and MDG-QA simultaneously). Since the Receiver Alias (MDG) remains constant, the pipeline now supports multicasting across virtual stages without requiring extra routing branches or modifications to Receiver Determination rules (they can be kept the same as in Production tenant).

Leveraging Authenticated Users for Dynamic Inbound Processing

Integration scenarios triggered from external sources (push-based) carry the SapAuthenticatedUserName header (available if it is added to the IFlow’s "Allowed Headers" list). This header contains the Client ID used by the caller. In SAP Integration Suite, a unique Client ID is generated for every Service Instance created via the BTP Cockpit.

Historically, the Pipeline determined the Scenario ID using the SAP_Sender (system name) and SAP_SenderInterface (interface name) headers. While effective, this model makes it difficult to build truly generic inbound processing steps for certain adapters.

The Challenge with Generic Inbound Adapters

Determining a sender interface dynamically is relatively straightforward (e.g., using XPath on an XML root or parsing the URI path in an HTTPS adapter). However, identifying the Sender System is much harder when that information isn't part of the payload or provided by standard adapter headers.

A prime example is the SOAP/SAP RM adapter. It lacks standard headers to identify the sender system and does not provide a unique URI path for the called service. Without a static header, dynamic sender determination becomes a technical hurdle.

The Solution: Identity-Based Determination

By supporting the SapAuthenticatedUserName header, the Pipeline can now implement Identity-Based Sender Determination. Instead of relying on manually populated headers (e.g. from externalized parameters), the Pipeline resolves the sender by mapping the caller's Client ID to a specific system or context in the Partner Directory.

This not only simplifies generic IFlow design but also provides a foundation for the Virtual Stages functionality. Two primary configuration patterns are now supported:

  1. Landscape Stage Resolution: Map an Authenticated User to a Partner ID using the Landscape_Stage~ prefix in the Partner Directory. This allows you to automatically identify the landscape stage (e.g., DEV, QA) across all scenarios based on which service key the sender uses.
  2. System-Specific Mapping: Map an Authenticated User directly to a Business System Partner ID. This identifies the exact business system via the caller's identity, effectively replacing the need for a static SAP_Sender header across multiple sender systems.

Architecture Overview

A single Integration Suite tenant can now host multiple parallel "stages" for your business systems (e.g., QA~Project, QA~Maintenance, Pre-Production, etc.).

To map your tenant to these Landscape Stages (also known as Virtual Stages), you can choose between two approaches:

  • Structured Assignment: Explicitly assign all possible virtual stages to the Partner SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape in the Partner Directory.
  • Dynamic Promotion: If a stage is supplied at runtime - via a header or the Authenticated User approach - that differs from the global configuration, the pipeline "promotes" this value into the current context.

The only prerequisite is that these Virtual Stages must be maintained in the Partner Directory for the respective Sender or Receiver System Aliases.

Core Logic: Identity & Scenario Resolution

The primary goal of the pipeline is to determine the Partner ID of an Integration Scenario. Traditionally, this relies on the SAP_Sender (Sender System name that is checked for System Alias existence) and SAP_SenderInterface headers. We have extended this logic to resolve the Virtual Stage and Sender Context before identifying the Scenario ID.

After running this resolution logic, the landscape stage could remain ambiguous (exact stage is unknown) - in this case multiple possible stages are transferred in the header tenantStage and the pipeline will try to navigate the required routing logic in the Receiver Stage determination.

The virtual stage routing follows a specific guardrail: the logic only executes if the header tenantStageVirtual is set to 'true'. This ensures that virtualization overhead never affects Production tenants. During Receiver Stage discovery, the system evaluates the target landscape. If the routing rules identify multiple valid virtual stages for a single Receiver Alias (only possible for Asynchronous processing), a Message Split occurs. The source message is then multicasted to all determined stages (e.g., both a Maintenance and a Project track). If only a single stage is identified, the tenantStage header is updated, and the message proceeds without multicasting.

Receiver Stage Discovery

The discovery process depends heavily on whether the Tenant Stage is already known and whether the Receiver is defined via a System Alias (abstraction) rather than a physical Business System.

Receiver Landscape Stage makes only sense for Receivers with maintained System Alias:

Scenario 1: The Tenant Stage is Known

When the Stage is identified, the system follows the 5-Step Routing Hierarchy:

  • Stage-Specific Rule Override: Use a specific rule to redirect the "Natural Choice" to a different stage. Stage-Specific Rule can be defined at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level.
  • Natural Choice: By default, route to the identical stage of the receiver (e.g., DEV to DEV).
  • Tenant-Level Fallback Rules: A Tenant-Wide rule maintained at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level, independent of the specific stage.

Scenario 2: The Tenant Stage is Unknown (Ambiguous)

If the Stage is unknown, the "Natural Choice" is unavailable. The system then evaluates:

  • Tenant-Level Rules: A Tenant-Wide rule maintained at the Configuration Scenario or at Receiver Alias level, independent of the specific stage.
  • Deterministic Exit: If the Receiver Alias has only one possible stage, the system auto-routes to that single destination.

To implement these rules, you must maintain String parameters in the Partner Directory using the following naming convention:

  • Parameter ID: stageRule_ + {Stage Name OR Tenant Name} + optional ~{Receiver Alias}.
  • Value Syntax: The character > followed by a comma-separated list of target stages.

Examples:

  • Stage-Specific with Receiver Alias: stageRule_DEV-MNT~S4HANA with value >DEV,QA-PRJ
  • Tenant-Wide without Receiver Alias: stageRule_My-IS-Tenant with value >QA-MNT

For a deep dive into the syntax and advanced configuration options, please refer to the Landscape Stages.

Sample Scenarios

In the following, we show you how to setup flexible stages and stage rules based on a couple of sample scenarios. Much more options are supported than shown here. For more details about all the options, see Landscape Stages.

As a prerequisite, we first need to create a partner ID SAP_Integration_Suite_Landscape in the Partner Directory where we maintain the list off all virtual stages as shown below. As you can see, the DEV tenant is mapped to two stages whereas the QA tenant is mapped to 3 stages. For PRD, we do not have virtual stages, here we have a 1:1 mapping.

Scenario passing message header to pipeline

In the first scenario, we simply pass the virtual stage via the message header tenantStage from the scenario-specific inbound processing flow to the generic flows of the pipeline. Furthermore, we have defined the sender system name, the sender interface name as well as the sender interface namespace in the scenario-specific inbound processing flow which is used to determine the configuration scenario. As you can see, we have externalized all parameters so that you can configure the parameters without need of changing the flow.

Because the stage name is passed via the message header, no other check is carried out to determine the stage. The pipeline then maps the sender business system, here BS_05E_S1QTST, and the corresponding stage, here QA-TST, to the sender alias, here BC_05E_S1. As you can see, we apply the following naming convention for the Partner Directory entries: We use the prefix BS (Business System) for the business system name and BC (Business Component) for the alias. Btw, for the configuration scenarios we use the prefix CS, see below.

Using the sender alias, we now determine the configuration scenario. For this, a corresponding alternative partner needs to be maintained in the Partner Directory.

Switching to the String Parameters, you notice that our example is a Point-to-Point scenario where we defined the receiver, here alias BC_05E_R1, and the end point of the scenario-specific outbound processing flow as string parameters. Furthermore, you can see that we have defined a stage rule for the stage QA-TST. In our case, when sending a message from the stage QA-TST, it should be duplicated and sent out to stages QA-PRJ and QA-TST.

Now that we have determined the receiver alias, we map the alias to the actual business systems via the corresponding Partner Directory entry.

When exchanging a message, the message processing log looks as follows.

Scenario using authorized user

In the second scenario, we use authorized users to determine the stage. Here, we have two options: either map an authorized user  to a partner ID with prefix Landscape_Stage or to a business system.

The alternative partner of this configuration scenario looks as follows. Here, we map the alias BC_05D_S1 to the configuration scenario.

Option 1

For this option, we pass sender system name (here BS_05D_S1DMNT), sender interface name and sender interface namespace to the pipeline. Furthermore, we use a particular service key to authenticate to the Cloud Integration runtime. This service key should only be used for systems of the stage DEV-MNT. We now map the client ID of the service key as authorized user to a partner ID with prefix Landscape_Stage, in this case Landscape_Stage~DEV-MNT. This way, we are able to directly determine the stage by fetching the prefix of the partner ID.

Furthermore, the business system BS_05D_S1DMNT is mapped to the alias BC_05D_S1 which is used in the configuration scenario above.

Like in the example before, we define a stage rule, this time for a specific receiver alias, here BC_05D_R1, and the stage DEV-MNT.

When exchanging a message, the message processing log looks as follows. The client ID is used to determine the stage. Using this very stage and the business system name, the system alias is determined and with this the configuration scenario and the receiver. The stage rule is applied and hence the message is sent to two receiving business systems.

Option 2

For this option, we do not pass any sender system name to the pipeline, instead we determine the sender business system and the virtual stage by matching an authorized user to a business system in the Partner Directory. Note, here we match the client ID of a dedicated service key to a business system name and not to an alias, in our case BS_05D_S1DPRJ.

On the String Parameters tab of the partner ID BS_05D_S1DPRJ, we have defined the stage DEV-PRJ as value of the ID landscapeStage. When authenticating to the Cloud Integration runtime using this very service key user, both are determined the business system name and the virtual stage, latter via the string parameter.

Furthermore, the business system BS_05D_S1DPRJ is mapped to the alias BC_05D_S1 which is used in the configuration scenario above.

This time, we haven't defined any stage rule. So, the message processing log then looks as follows.

 

Conclusion and Outlook

 

The concept of Virtual Stages has been a consistent objective since the initial introduction of Landscape Stages. Finding the balance between robust functionality, feature clarity, and configuration simplicity was the primary architectural challenge during development. Because these changes impact the core pipeline routing engine, our priority was ensuring stability and avoiding regressions while incorporating these new configuration options.

It is important to note a current limitation: Virtual Stage Routing currently requires that a single tenant share the same Configuration Scenario across all its stages.

In practice, there is often a need for version divergence. For example, consider an existing point-to-point interface. If a change request requires adding a new receiver, you might want to transition that interface to use Receiver Determination in the Development stage while maintaining the original Point-to-Point logic in the Quality Assurance stage.

Currently, this "cross-version" setup is not yet supported because Scenario ID determination relies exclusively on the Sender and the Sender Interface. The Stage Context is not yet a factor in identifying the Configuration Scenario.

However, this is the intended direction for the framework. Integrating stage-awareness into the scenario determination logic is a planned update for upcoming increments. For now, this version provides a functional foundation for any landscape where the Configuration Scenario remains consistent across all tracks.

entry.title: Enhanced IDoc inbound support for Cloud Integration Pipeline
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the release of version 1.0.7 of the Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound package with major enhancements of the Pipeline v2 Generic Step01 - Inbound Processing for Idoc flow. This enhancements include support for partner type not equal LS and support for so-called virtual receiver in order to match the incoming IDoc message to a configuration scenario partner ID in the Partner Directory.

In order to determine the partner ID of the configuration scenario, the generic IDoc inbound processing flow checks if an alternative partner exists that matches the sender information, the sender interface name and eventually the sender interface namespace derived from the IDoc control header of the incoming IDoc message.

The sender interface name is a combination of the message type MESTYP, the IDoc type IDOCTYP, and optionally, if not empty, the extension type CIMTYP. The namespace can be taken into account or not. In our examples below, we use it.

The agency of the alternative partner is derived from the sender information of the IDoc control header. Its definition however depends on the partner type, i.e., whether it's a logical system (LS) or not (like LI, KU, etc.), and whether landscape stages (e.g., DEV, QA, PRD or similar) are used or not. See Landscape Stages. Furthermore, you have the option to use a so-called virtual receiver. In this case, both the sender partner information and the receiver partner information provided in the IDoc control record is considered to determine the partner ID of the configuration scenario.

Btw, in order to include the check for the virtual receiver, you need to configure the externalized parameter CustomXPid_ResolveVirtualReceiver of the generic IDoc inbound processing flow to true. By default, it's false hence disabled to reduce the number of overall checks assuming that the usage of a virtual receiver is not frequently used. If the virtual receiver is enabled, we first check if we can find a match including the receiver information. In case that no match is found, we then check if only the sender matches. This rule is especially discussed in the very last sample scenario below.

For a detailed description of all configuration options, see Determine Partner ID for IDoc Inbound.

In the following, I will go through some sample configurations to better understand the new features and configuration options when it comes to IDoc inbound scenarios. For most of the scenarios we keep it simple and just have configured the Point-to-Point pattern.

Furthermore, we stick to the definition of the Agency in the alternative partner. For all scenarios the Scheme is defined as combination of message type FLIGHTBOOKING_CREATEFROMDAT and IDoc type FLIGHTBOOKING_CREATEFROMDAT01, and the ID equals urn:sap-com:document:sap:idoc:messages.

Incoming IDoc with sender partner type = LS, no usage of landscape stages

In this sample scenario, we do not use landscape stage and the sender partner type SNDPRT of the incoming IDoc message equals LS. In this case, the sender partner number SNDPRN equals the logical system name of the sender. So, on the tab Alternative Partners of the configuration scenario we define the Agency equals the partner number SNDPRN, in our case O6DCLNT607.

As said, in most cases we simply configure a Point-to-Point scenario. So, on the String Parameters tab we define the receiver and the end point of the scenario-specific outbound processing flow as follows:

When exchanging an IDoc using the pipeline for Cloud Integration, the message processing log of the generic IDoc inbound processing flow has been enhanced to provide further details about the IDoc sender partner, the partner ID of the configuration scenario determined and the alternative partner which at the end matches to the partner ID. In this case, we send an IDoc of sender partner type LS and sender partner number O6DCLNT607. So, you can see from the Custom Headers section of the log that the partner number O6DCLNT607 led to the configuration scenario CS_PIP_Samples_Scenario_06D. The IDoc sender partner is defined as combination of the sender partner type, the sender partner number and the sender port, here LS.O6DCLNT607.SAPO6D.

Sender partner type = LS, virtual receiver ≠ LS, without landscape stages

In the next sample scenario, like in the example before we do not use landscape stage and the sender partner type SNDPRT of the incoming IDoc message equals LS. This time however, we use a virtual receiver not equal LS. The Agency is then defined as a combination of the logical system name of the sender and a representation of the receiver party separated by a colon ":".

The receiver party follows the pattern ALE#<RCVPRT>[#<RCVPFC>]#<RCVPRN> with <RCVPRT> the value of the receiver partner type, <RCVPFC> the value of the receiver partner function, and <RCVPRN> the receiver partner number.

In our example, the sender partner number SNDPRN equals J6QCLNT800. In the sending business system, the receiver partner is defined as sold-to-party customer (partner type KU and partner function SP) with customer number 9934567890. The Agency is then defined as follows: J6QCLNT800:ALE#KU#SP#9934567890.

When exchanging an IDoc fitting to the configuration scenario, the message processing log contains all relevant details like seen above. This time, you also get the IDoc receiver partner displayed as combination of the receiver partner type, the receiver partner function, the receiver partner number and the receiver port.

Sender partner type ≠ LS, virtual receiver ≠ LS, with landscape stages

Now, let's look at a configuration using landscape stage. When using landscape stages, we usually map the actual business system name to a system alias. Actually, because we exchange IDoc messages here, we rather map the logical system name to the alias. To determine the partner ID and the receivers we only use alias names within the pipeline. This way, the configuration scenario can be defined independent of the landscape stage, e.g., the XSLT containing the XPath conditions to determining the receivers and interfaces contains alias only and hence doesn't have to be modified when being used across the different tenants. See Landscape Stages.

In this sample scenario, we again use virtual receiver. Both, the sender partner type and the receiver partner type are not equal LS.

Assuming, we test the scenario in the QA landscape. The sender business system in QA and the sender partner are defined as follows:

  • Sender business system name equals BS_06K_S1Q.
  • Sender partner type SNDPRT equals KU.
  • Sender partner number SNDPRN equals 0000112015.
  • Sender port SNDPOR equals SAPK6Q.
  • System client MANDT equals 400.

In the sender business system, the receiver partner is defined as follows:

  • Receiver partner type RCVPRT equals LI.
  • Receiver partner number RCVPRN equals 0000012935.
  • Receiver port RCVPOR equals A000000012.

Because the sender partner type is not a logical system name, we first need to derive the logical system name of the sender from the sender port and the client. We only support sender ports that follow the pattern SAP<SID> with <SID> the system ID, here K6Q. This results into the logical system name K6QCLNT400 which is then mapped to a partner ID representing the alias of the sender system, here BC_06K_S1. You may have noticed that we apply the following naming convention for the Partner Directory entries: We use the prefix BS (Business System) for the business system name and BC (Business Component) for the alias. For the configuration scenarios we actually use the prefix CS.

For partners of type not equal LS, we can map the IDoc control record fields that describe the partner to a party alias. Usually, you choose this option when using landscape stages. So, for the sender we create a partner in the Partner Directory representing the sender party, here PRTY_06K_Blue. The alternative partner to map to this party is then defined as follows:

  • Agency: Name of the sender alias, here BC_06K_S1.
  • Scheme: ALE#<SNDPRT>[#<SNDPFC>], here ALE#KU.
  • ID: <SNDPRN>, here 0000112015.

The same applies for the receiver party. Here, we create a new partner PRTY_06K_Amber in the Partner Directory with the following alternative partner:

  • Agency: Name of the sender alias, here BC_06K_S1.
  • Scheme: ALE#<RCVPRT>[#<RCVPFC>], here ALE#LI.
  • ID: <RCVPRN>, here 0000012935.

Now let's take a look at the alternative partner to match the sender and the virtual receiver to the configuration scenario. Like in the example before, the Agency is then defined as a combination of a sender part and a receiver part separated by a colon ":".

For the sender part, we apply the same pattern like we do for party support in the pipeline in general, i.e., we combine the party alias and the system alias separated by a period, ".", in our case PRTY_06K_Blue.BS_06K_S1.

For the receiver part, we stick to the party alias only, here PRTY_06K_Amber.

This results into the Agency PRTY_06K_Blue.BS_06K_S1:PRTY_06K_Amber.

When exchanging an IDoc fitting to the configuration scenario, the message processing log contains all relevant details like seen above.

Sender partner type = LS, virtual receiver ≠ LS, with fallback configuration, with landscape stages

Our last example is the most complex one. This example actually comprises multiple configuration scenarios. We send out IDoc messages from a business system to multiple partners. The sender partner type equals LS whereas the receiver partner type for all receiver partners is KU with partner function AG. For some partners, individual configuration scenarios exist using virtual receiver. Those are of pattern Point-to-Point. Where such a match is not found, we then check for a match without virtual receiver and have defined a sort of fallback configuration scenario. The fallback configuration scenario is of pattern Content-Based-Router where an XSLT mapping is carried out to determine the receiver.

We use landscape stages, so we first map the logical system name of the sender M6QCLNT107 to a partner ID representing the alias of the sender system, here BC_06M_S1.

Furthermore, we map the partner numbers of the receiver partners to party aliases in the Partner Directory, here partner number 0000077891 with scheme ALE#KU#AG is mapped to party alias PRTY_06M_White.

Similarly for PRTY_06M_Grey. Here partner number 0000077892 with scheme ALE#KU#AG is mapped to party alias PRTY_06M_Grey.

In the configuration scenario for the receiver-specific configuration scenario for the party PRTY_06M_White, the Agency of the alternative partner is defined as a combination of the sender alias and the receiver party alias: BC_06M_S1.PRTY_06M_White.

As said, the receiver-specific configuration scenarios are of pattern Point-to-Point where we define receiver and end point of the receiver-specific outbound processing flow as string parameters.

The configuration scenario for the receiver-specific configuration scenario for the party PRTY_06M_Grey is defined in a similar way. The Agency of the alternative partner is defined as follows: BC_06M_S1.PRTY_06M_Grey.

The fallback configuration scenario kicks in if a potential virtual receiver won't match to a receiver-specific configuration scenario. In this case, in a second attempt, the IDoc inbound processing flow checks for a match using the sender alias nonly. Hence the Agency of the fallback configuration scenario is defined as sender alias BC_06M_S1.

On the Binary Parameters tab, an XSLT needs to be uploaded with ID receiverDetermination which is carried out to determine the receiver.

The XSLT looks as follows. The XPaths actually depend on the receiver partner numbers from the IDoc control record of the incoming messages.

So, let's now send an IDoc message for the receiver with customer number 0000077891 to the pipeline. As you can see from the message processing log, the receiver-specific configuration scenario for the party PRTY_06M_White has been determined.

When we send an IDoc message for the receiver with customer number 0000077893 to the pipeline, the fallback configuration scenario has been determined.

Hope those sample scenarios help you to better understand the configuration options for IDoc inbound scenarios using the new features in the pipeline for Cloud Integration.

As we have used landscape stages in some of the sample scenarios, you may be curious about the latest enhancements that we have shipped to improve the support for landscape stages. See Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline.

entry.title: New features in the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages with the March 2026 shipment
entry.content:
We have just shipped new versions of the Pipeline for Cloud Integration packages. This time we implemented two major features, namely support for more flexible landscape stages and major changes in the generic IDoc inbound processing flow. The following new versions have been shipped:

  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Inbound version 1.0.7
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Pipelines version 1.0.4
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Generic Outbound version 1.0.3
  • Cloud Integration Pipeline - Templates version 1.1.1

The following new features have been shipped at a glance:

  • Supporting IDocs with partner type other than LS such as LI or KU. Before, we only supported partner type LS. Furthermore, you have the option to include a so called virtual receiver to determine the partner ID similar to how we support IDoc inbound scenarios in SAP Process Orchestration. For more details, see Determine Partner ID for IDoc Inbound. Furthermore, check out this blog post Enhanced IDoc inbound support for Cloud Integration Pipeline.
  • Support for more flexible landscape stages taking into account different landscape stages of your business systems compared to the number of SAP Integration Suite tenants. E.g., having only two tenants one for dev and qa and the other for prod. So, you have the option to define multiple stages per tenant and to define so called stage rules in case that you like to route a message from one stage to another. Furthermore, different options to determine the stage are supported: via authorized user, via header passed to the pipeline steps or via configuration parameter in the Partner Directory. For more details, see Landscape Stages. Furthermore, check out this blog post Virtual Landscape Stages for Cloud Integration Pipeline.
  • For the generic XI outbound flow, we now support dynamic headers like we already do for the generic XI inbound. See Dynamic Headers chapter in XI Sender Adapter and XI Receiver Adapter.
  • For the generic XI outbound flow, you can now override the headers SAP_Sender and SAP_Receiver from the default externalized parameters. If the scenario-specific inbound processing flow passes empty header values to the generic XI outbound flow, the values of the externalized parameters are used. See very last note at XI Receiver Adapter.
  • The generic IDoc outbound flow has been enhanced to display the partner functions SNDPFC and RCVPFC in the custom header properties section of the message processing log in message monitoring in addition to partner type, partner port and partner number.
  • The generic IDoc outbound flow now fails if any mandatory control record field is empty or missing. Before, this was ignored which however would have led to an error in the backend systems.
  • We fixed an issue with a wrong type in a content modifier of the template Pipeline v2 Template Step01 - Inbound Processing Synchronous in the templates package, it was Constant before, now the correct type is Expression.

Please update the packages in your Design area to benefit from the changes. Though we run extensive regression tests and new feature tests before publishing a new version, we nevertheless recommend to update your dev and test landscape first and to test there before updating the content on your prod tenant.

entry.title: Streamlining SAP CPI Migration: A Postman-Driven Guide from Neo to Cloud Foundry
entry.content:
CPI Migration: A Postman-Driven Guide from Neo to Cloud Foundry

Introduction

Migrating from the Neo environment to Cloud Foundry (CF) is not just a technical necessity. It is also a chance to review and improve your integration landscape. While SAP provides the Migration Tooling, using Postman collections with the OData API gives you a detailed, repeatable, and clear method to transfer your artifacts. Using Postman collections with CPI APIs can automate the migration process and boost productivity.

Pre-requisite: 1. Identify the Correct Service Plan

Make sure after creating the sub account, respective service plan has to be created in the Integration suite.

2. Create a Service Instance

Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions and create a new instance of the Process Integration Runtime.

Service: Process Integration Runtime Plan: api, integration-flow Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry

3. Assign Necessary Roles (JSON Configuration)

During instance creation, you need to define the authorities. Use a JSON structure to grant the roles needed for migration, such as: 1. WorkspacePackagesRead or WorkspacePackagesWrite 2. KeyStoreRead or KeyStoreWrite 3. AuthGroup_IntegrationDeveloper 4. AuthGroup.Administrator

4. Generate the Service Key

After INSTANCE is created, click the Create service key. This will generate a JSON block containing the:

l ClientId

l ClientSecret

l Token URL

Migration Steps Using Postman

 

CPI MIG010 Connect to Tenants

CPI MIG020 Readiness Checks

CPI MIG030 Variables

CPI MIG050 Custom Tags

CPI MIG060 Custom Public Certificates

CPI MIG070 User Credentials

CPI MIG080 OAuth2 Client Credentials

CPI MIG110 Pre-Packaged Content

CPI MIG120 Custom Content

CPI MIG130 Value Mapping Values

 

 

 

Step-by-Step Migration Workflow

 

 

1. Establishing the Foundation (MIG010 - MIG030)

Before migrating, you must establish a secure handshake between your environments. CPI MIG010 Connect to Tenants: This is your starting point. You'll set up your Environment Variables in Postman to store the Host URLs and OAuth tokens for both Neo (Source) and CF (Target).

 

 

CPI MIG020 Readiness Checks: This step uses APIs to check if the target Cloud Foundry tenant is active and has the required service plans, specifically Process Integration Runtime, assigned.

 

CPI MIG030 Variables: Many iflows use Write Variables. This collection makes sure that persistent data stored in the Neo database is captured and ready to be recreated in the CF tenant.

 

2. Migrating Security and Identity (MIG050 - MIG080)

Security artifacts are often the overlooked elements that lead to migration failures during testing.

CPI MIG050 Custom Tags: Transfer your metadata. Custom tags help you organize and search for iFlows. Keeping these consistent ensures your governance model remains intact.

CPI MIG060 Custom Public Certificates: Move your X.509 certificates. Make sure to export the public keys from Neo and post them to the CF Keystore. This helps maintain trust with external partners.

CPI MIG070 and MIG080 Credentials: Whether you are using User Credentials (Basic Auth) or OAuth2 Client Credentials, these steps automate the creation of security material entries. 3. Content Transfer (MIG110, MIG130)

This is the key part of the migration: moving the integration logic.

CPI MIG110 Pre-Packaged Content: For standard SAP content, this ensures you are subscribed to the latest versions in the CF Business Accelerator Hub.

CPI MIG120 Custom Content: This is the main phase. This collection transfers the .zip bundles of your custom iFlows from Neo to the CF Design workspace.

CPI MIG130 Value Mapping: Value mappings often have thousands of entries. Using the API to migrate these is much faster and less prone to errors than manual exports.

Post-Migration activity :

Monitor : Monitor the integration suite closely all the iflows for more than 1 week.

Clear the Cache: After migrating certificates (MIG060), give the CF runtime a few minutes to refresh the keystore.

 

Conclusion : If you're doing a Neo migration over to Cloud Foundry, using Postman collections could really change things up. It turns that whole manual setup, which is full of mistakes and takes forever, into something more organized. You know, repeatable steps that you can just run again without hassle, and even automate parts of it. That seems helpful for keeping the Integration Suite clean, I guess. No mess left behind.

entry.title: Centralized Error Handling in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
Introduction

In SAP Cloud Integration, runtime errors can occur due to various reasons such as script failures, adapter issues, or unexpected payloads. Handling such errors individually in every integration flow often leads to duplicated logic and increased maintenance effort.

This blog demonstrates a centralized error handling mechanism in SAP Cloud Integration that:

  • Captures errors using an Exception Subprocess
  • Forwards error data using the ProcessDirect adapter
  • Sends HTML-formatted email notifications
  • Is reusable across multiple business integration flows

Solution

The solution consists of two types of integration flows:

1. Business Integration Flows

  • Executes business logic
  • Uses an Exception Subprocess to capture errors
  • Sends error details to a centralized logger

2. Central Error Logger Integration Flows

  • Receives error messages from multiple iFlows
  • Normalizes error data
  • Sends styled HTML email notifications to the support team

Step 1: Create the Business Integration Flow

1. Navigate to the SAP Integration Suite

2. Go to Design → Integrations and APIs

3. Create a new package (example: PCK_CentralErrorLogging)

4. Create a new Integration Flow named Business_Test_Iflow

This iFlow represents a sample business process where a runtime error occurs.

 

Step 2: Configure the Sender and Main Process

1. Add an HTTPS Sender Adapter

  • Configure endpoint address (example: /testError)
  • Configure authentication as required

2. Connect the adapter to the Start Event

3. Add a Groovy Script named Script_Groovy_Generate_Error

  • This script deliberately throws an exception for testing purposes

4. Connect the script to the End Event

 

Step 3: Add Exception Subprocess for Error Capture

1. Add an Exception Subprocess to the Business iFlow

2. Add a Groovy Script inside the subprocess named Script_Groovy_CaptureError

The Groovy script performs the following responsibilities:

  • Capture integration flow name
  • Capture message ID
  • Generate correlation ID
  • Capture error message
  • Generate timestamps (IST and UTC)

All values are stored as message properties. Using an Exception Subprocess enables centralized error capture without modifying individual processing steps.

 

Step 4: Send Error Details via ProcessDirect Adapter

1. After the Groovy script, add a ProcessDirect Receiver Adapter

2. Configure the address as /centralErrorLogger

This enables reusability across multiple business iFlows.

Step 5: Create the Central Error Logger Integration Flow

1. Create an integration flow named Central_Error_Logger_Iflow

2. Configure the Sender Adapter as ProcessDirect

3. Use the same address /centralErrorLogger

Step 6: Normalize Incoming Error Data

1. Add a Groovy Script as the first step

2. The script reads error details from properties, headers, or payload and applies fallback values if data is missing, preparing a consistent structure for email formatting.

This makes the central logger reusable.

Step 7: Format Error Details into HTML

1. Add a Content Modifier

2. Create an HTML table displaying:

  • Integration Flow Name
  • Message ID
  • Adapter Type
  • Error Message
  • Timestamp (IST and UTC)
  • Correlation ID

 

Step 8: Configure Email Notification

1. Add a Mail Receiver Adapter

2. Configure mail server, sender and receiver email IDs, subject, and set MIME type to text/html

Step 9: End-to-End Testing

1. Deploy both integration flows

2. Trigger the business iFlow using the HTTP endpoint

3. Verify the following:

  • Business iFlow succeeds in Message Monitor
  • Central Error Logger receives the error
  • HTML email is delivered with error details

 

Validation Using Another Business Integration Flow

The same centralized error handling was validated with another real-world integration flow named Invoice_Batch_Email_Processor_With_ProcessDirect.

  • Multiple processing steps
  • Routers based on message severity
  • Multiple email notifications
  • Groovy scripts and content modifiers

 

Key Observations

  • Exception Subprocess captured runtime errors successfully
  • The same central error logger iFlow was reused
  • No changes were required in the logger
  • HTML email alerts were generated correctly

 

This confirms the solution works for both simple and complex integration flows.

Benefits of This Approach

  • Centralized error handling
  • Reusable across multiple iFlows
  • Clean separation of business logic and error handling
  • Faster troubleshooting using Message ID and Correlation ID
  • Professional HTML email notifications
  • Proven in real-world scenarios

Conclusion

By implementing centralized error handling using Exception Subprocess and ProcessDirect adapters, SAP Cloud Integration landscapes become easier to maintain, monitor, and support. This approach reduces duplication, improves observability, and scales efficiently across integration designs.

This solution was implemented and tested in SAP Integration Suite, and all screenshots are from real execution to ensure accuracy and practical relevance.

entry.title: Modern UI Email Weather Alerts with SAP Integration Suite — Automated Threshold‑Based Notification
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

The business conditions are very dependent on the current weather situation. In industries like logistics, transportation, field service and others, changes in temperature or even harsh weather conditions will affect the daily operation and employee security. In order to prevent this, there is a need for a automated and reliable solution to monitor the conditions and receive a notification on scheduled basis, when certain levels are crossed (e.g. Extreme heat, cold, freezing conditions etc.).

BUSINESS SCENARIO

The purpose of this blog is to show how SAP Integration Suite (CPI) can be used to automate weather tracking and send alerts based on predefined temperature thresholds. The iFlow evaluates current weather data, identifies unusual temperature conditions, and triggers polished, user‑friendly email notifications.
WHAT THE IFLOW DOES
  • Automatically triggers on a configured schedule using a Timer Start Event.
  • Fetches real-time weather data from OpenWeather for a configurable city.
  • Classifies temperature using a Groovy-based logic with the following categories:
    • Extreme Hot → when temperature is 40°C or above
    • Hot → when temperature is between 35°C and 39°C
    • Warm → when temperature is between 26°C and 34°C
    • Normal → when temperature is between 18°C and 25°C
    • Cool → when temperature is between 10°C and 17°C
    • Cold → when temperature is between 1°C and 9°C
    • Freezing → when temperature is 0°C or below
    • Unknown → when no valid temperature value is received
  • Determines routing behavior:
    • If Normal → No email is sent.
    • If Abnormal → A polished HTML email alert is generated and sent via the Mail Adapter.
  • Uses secure email authentication, such as a Gmail App Password stored in CPI Security Material.

IMPLEMENTATION FLOW OVERVIEW

This diagram presents a high‑level overview of the complete implementation flow for creating the Scheduled Weather Alert iFlow in SAP Integration Suite. It highlights each major phase—from initial setup (API key configuration and email credential creation) to package setup, and the key iFlow components such as the Timer event, Content Modifier, HTTP integration, Groovy processing, Router logic, and Mail adapter. It also illustrates the final stages of deployment and monitoring within the Integration Suite.

PREREQUISITES

  • An active SAP BTP trial account with Integration Suite subscription and corresponding roles assigned to it.   
Note: For guidance on creating a SAP BTP trial account, refer to Get an Account on SAP BTP Trial To activate and subscribe to the Integration Suite service, see Set Up Integration Suite Trial
  • OpenWeather API key (Free/paid; One Call or Current Weather endpoint).OpenWeather API ) 
  • Gmail App Password (if using Gmail SMTP). 
Steps to Create an OpenWeather API
  • Open the Website
    • Go to: OpenWeather
  • Create a Free Account
    • Click Sign Up (top-right)
    • Enter your email, password
    • Verify your email via the link sent to your inbox
    • After verification, log in using your credentials
  • Choose Weather API
    • Use the Current Weather Data API
https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather?q={city name},{country code}&appid={API key} 
  • Go to “My API Keys”
    • Click your profile icon
    • Select My API Keys

 

You will see a default API key
Copy The API Key for later use
 
Steps to create Mail Credentials — Gmail App Password & Security Material 
  •  Generate Gmail App Password
    • Enable 2-Step Verification in your Google Account
    • Search For → App passwords. 
    • Create a new app password (e.g., Mail)
    • Copy the 16-character App Password (keep it safe).
  •  Add CPI Security Material 
    • Go to your Integration Suite
    • Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Manage Security Material → Create → User Credentials

    • Type: User Credentials 
    • Name: GmailCredential
    • User: your Gmail address (the same email used to generate the App Password) 
    • Password: the App Password generated earlier.

 

  • Connectivity Test (SMTP – Gmail)  
    • Go to your Integration Suite
    • Go to Monitor → Integrations and APIs → Connectivity Test

    • Choose SMTP as the protocol 
    • Fill in the following details: 
      • Host: smtp.gmail.com 
      • Port: 587
      • Authentication: Plain User/Password 
      • Credential Name: Select the Gmail User Credential created earlier in Security Material 
      • Click Send  
        • If successful → Your SMTP setup is correct 
        • If error → Review the error message and reconfigure the settings accordingly 

DESIGNING THE IFLOW

STEPS 1: Create an Integration Package 
  • Open SAP Integration Suite → Design → Integrations & APIs. 
  • Click Create
  • Fill Package Details
    • Name: Weather Forecast Alert 
    • Short Description: Automated Weather Alert System using OpenWeather API and Email Notification.
    • Save the package
STEPS 2: Create a New Integration Flow 
  • Add Artifact - Inside your package:  
  • Click Add → Integration Flow.
  • Fill Iflow Details
    • Name: IFL_WeatherAlert 
    • Description:
      • Interface Type - Synchronous 
      • Overall Flow - Timer → SAP CPI → OpenWeather API → Mail 
      • Receiver Method - GET 
    • Click Add and Open in Editor
STEPS 3: Timer Event 
To schedule the iFlow so it automatically triggers at fixed intervals or at a specific time, ensuring weather data is fetched and processed without manual execution.
  • Drag Start Timer from palette. 

  • Scheduler → Configuration: 
  • Default Value: Choose Basic
  • Frequency: 
    • Enter As: Simple Schedule
    • Repeat: Repeat Daily 
    • Every: 1 day
  • Time Range:
    • Start Date & Time: set required start (e.g., 2026‑03‑13 11:22)
    • End Date & Time: optional
  • Time Zone: Use UTC +5:30 — India Standard Time (Asia/Kolkata) or choose the time zone required for your business scenario
  • Throw Exception on Schedule Expiry: Enable if needed ✔  

STEPS 4: Content Modifier — Set Parameters

To prepare and organize message data by setting required properties, headers, or body content. 

  • Drag Content Modifier into flow. 
  • Go to the Exchange Property Tab.
  • Add Properties: 
    • Name: cityName
      Source Type: Constant
      Source Value: Hyderabad
    • Name: countryCode
      Source Type: Constant
      Source Value: IN
    • Name: appid
      Source Type: Constant
      Source Value: Your OpenWeather API Key 
STEPS 5: Request‑Reply 
The Request‑Reply step is used when an iFlow must call an external API and wait for its response before moving forward. In this use case, SAP CPI sends a request to the OpenWeather API, receives the weather data in real time, and then uses that response to format the output and trigger the email notification.
  • Drag Request‑Reply into the flow (place it after the Content Modifier where you build your API request).
STEPS 5: Receiver
  • Drag a Receiver from the palette onto the iFlow. 
  • Rename it to OpenWeatherConnect 
    (This name will help you identify that it is used for the weather API call.) 
  • Draw a connector from the Request‑Reply step to OpenWeatherConnect. 
  • When prompted for adapter selection, choose HTTP Adapter.

Configure HTTP Adapter (Receiver → OpenWeatherConnect) 

  • Address: Provide the OpenWeather API Base URL 
    https://api.openweathermap.org/data/2.5/weather 
  • Query Parameters: Will be added inside Content Modifier or Request‑Reply earlie
q=${property.cityName},${property.countryCode}&appid=${property.appid} 
  • Method: GET 
  • Authentication: None 
  • Request Headers: *  

STEPS 6: Groovy Script — Classify & Extract

Parse the OpenWeather JSON response, extract important fields (city, country, temp, temp_min, temp_max, humidity, wind speed, timestamp), classify the temperature into COLD / WARM / HOT, and set the required properties for the next steps such as routing and email formatting.  

  • Drag a Groovy Script right after the Request‑Reply. 
  • Add New Script → Click + icon to add 
  • Paste the script below.
import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import groovy.json.JsonSlurper
import java.time.format.DateTimeFormatter
import java.math.RoundingMode
import java.time.*
import java.io.Reader

Message processData(Message message) {

    def bodyObj = message.getBody(java.io.Reader) 
    def json = new JsonSlurper().parse(bodyObj)
    
    
    def toC = { BigDecimal v ->
        if (v == null) return null
        BigDecimal c = (v > 200) ? (v - 273.15G) : v
        c.setScale(1, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)
    }

    def toCardinal = { Integer deg ->
        if (deg == null) return "N/A"
        String[] dirs = ["N","NNE","NE","ENE","E","ESE","SE","SSE","S","SSW","SW","WSW","W","WNW","NW","NNW"]
        int idx = Math.floor(((deg % 360) / 22.5) + 0.5) as int
        dirs[idx % 16]
    }

    def classify = { BigDecimal c ->
        if (c == null) return "Unknown"
        if (c >= 40)       "Extreme Heat"
        else if (c >= 35)  "Hot"
        else if (c >= 26)  "Warm"
        else if (c >= 18)  "Normal"
        else if (c >= 10)  "Cool"
        else if (c >= 1)   "Cold"
        else               "Freezing"
    }

    def fmtLocalDateTime = { Long epochSec, ZoneOffset z, DateTimeFormatter fmt ->
        try { (epochSec ? ZonedDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochSecond(epochSec), z).format(fmt) : "N/A") }
        catch (Exception e) { "N/A" }
    }
    def fmtLocalTime = { Long epochSec, ZoneOffset z, DateTimeFormatter fmt ->
        try { (epochSec ? ZonedDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochSecond(epochSec), z).format(fmt) : "N/A") }
        catch (Exception e) { "N/A" }
    }
    def fmtLocalDate = { Long epochSec, ZoneOffset z, DateTimeFormatter fmt ->
        try { (epochSec ? ZonedDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochSecond(epochSec), z).format(fmt) : "N/A") }
        catch (Exception e) { "N/A" }
    }

    def categoryColor = { String cat ->
        switch (cat) {
            case "Extreme Heat": return "#b91c1c"
            case "Hot":          return "#ef4444"
            case "Warm":         return "#f59e0b"
            case "Normal":       return "#10b981"
            case "Cool":         return "#3b82f6"
            case "Cold":         return "#2563eb"
            case "Freezing":     return "#0ea5e9"
            default:             return "#111827"

        }
    }

    // -------- 3) Extract fields --------
    String city        = (json?.name ?: "Unknown") as String
    String country     = (json?.sys?.country ?: "") as String
    String loc         = country ? "${city}, ${country}" : city
    String condition   = (json?.weather && json.weather.size() > 0) ? (json.weather[0]?.main ?: "N/A") : "N/A"
    String description = (json?.weather && json.weather.size() > 0) ? (json.weather[0]?.description ?: "N/A") : "N/A"

    BigDecimal tempRaw   = (json?.main?.temp       ?: 0) as BigDecimal
    BigDecimal feelsRaw  = (json?.main?.feels_like ?: 0) as BigDecimal
    Integer humidity     = (json?.main?.humidity   ?: 0) as Integer
    Integer pressure     = (json?.main?.pressure   ?: 0) as Integer
    Integer visibilityM  = (json?.visibility       ?: 0) as Integer
    BigDecimal windSpeed = (json?.wind?.speed      ?: 0) as BigDecimal
    Integer windDeg      = (json?.wind?.deg        ?: 0) as Integer
    Integer cloudCover   = (json?.clouds?.all      ?: 0) as Integer

    Long fetchTs   = (json?.dt ?: 0L) as Long
    Long sunriseTs = (json?.sys?.sunrise ?: 0L) as Long
    Long sunsetTs  = (json?.sys?.sunset  ?: 0L) as Long

    Integer tzOffsetSec = (json?.timezone ?: 0) as Integer
    ZoneOffset zoneOffset = ZoneOffset.ofTotalSeconds(tzOffsetSec)

    // -------- 4) Conversions & derived --------
    BigDecimal tempC = toC(tempRaw)
    BigDecimal feelC = toC(feelsRaw)
    String windDir   = toCardinal(windDeg)
    String visibilityKm = (visibilityM / 1000.0).toBigDecimal().setScale(1, RoundingMode.HALF_UP).toPlainString()
    String category  = classify(tempC)

    DateTimeFormatter dateFmt     = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("EEE, dd MMM yyyy")
    DateTimeFormatter timeFmt     = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("hh:mm a")
    DateTimeFormatter dateTimeFmt = DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("EEE, dd MMM yyyy • hh:mm a")

    String fetchedAtLocal = fmtLocalDateTime(fetchTs, zoneOffset, dateTimeFmt)
    String sunriseLocal   = fmtLocalTime(sunriseTs,  zoneOffset, timeFmt)
    String sunsetLocal    = fmtLocalTime(sunsetTs,   zoneOffset, timeFmt)
    String localDate      = fmtLocalDate(fetchTs,    zoneOffset, dateFmt)

    String tzReadable = zoneOffset.getId()
    if (tzReadable == "Z") tzReadable = "+00:00"

    String color = categoryColor(category)

    // -------- 5) Set properties for Mail / templates --------
    message.setProperty("Weather.City",            city)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Country",         country)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Location",        loc)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Condition",       condition)
    message.setProperty("Weather.Description",     description)
    message.setProperty("Weather.TempC",           "${tempC}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.FeelsLikeC",      "${feelC}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.Humidity",        "${humidity}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.Pressure",        "${pressure}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.VisibilityKm",    visibilityKm)
    message.setProperty("Weather.WindSpeedMS",     "${windSpeed}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.WindDeg",         "${windDeg}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.WindDir",         windDir)
    message.setProperty("Weather.CloudsPct",       "${cloudCover}")
    message.setProperty("Weather.Category",        category)

    message.setProperty("Weather.TimezoneOffset",  tzReadable)
    message.setProperty("Weather.FetchedAtLocal",  fetchedAtLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.SunriseLocal",    sunriseLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.SunsetLocal",     sunsetLocal)
    message.setProperty("Weather.LocalDate",       localDate)
    message.setProperty("Weather.CategoryColor",   color)
    
    return message
}
  • Click Apply

STEPS 7: Router — Normal vs Default

To evaluate weather severity and decide whether to send an email alert or stop the flow.

  • Drag the Router into the flow.Place it after Groovy

  • Create Two Branches 
    • Branch 1 — Normal Weather (No Email)
      • Click on the first branch line.
      • Add the condition:
      • Expression Type: Non-XML
        • Condition: ${property.Weather.Category} = 'Normal'
        • Connect this branch directly to the End Event. This stops the flow and no email is sent.
    • Branch 2 — Default Branch (Send Email)
      • Click on the second branch.
      • Mark it as Default Route.
      • This path should connect to your End Message → Mail Sender.

This ensures that we only send alerts when the weather crosses defined thresholds.

STEPS 7: Mail Receiver

  • Drag a Receiver to the canvas and connect it to the End Message. 
  • Rename it to Mail. 
    (This name will help you identify that it is used for the weather API call.) 
  • Draw a connector from the End Message step to Mail. 
  • When prompted for adapter selection, choose MAIL Adapter.

Mail Receiver — HTML Email with Modern UI 

  • Connection Tab
    • Address: smtp.gmail.com:587 
    • Authentication: Plain User/Password 
    • Credential Name: Enter the User Credential you created earlier in the Security Material.

  • Processing Tab
    • From: Your Gmail ID (the same email used to generate the App Password) 
    • To: Receiver email address 
    • Subject: 
Weather • ${property.Weather.Location}: ${property.Weather.TempC}°C (${property.Weather.Category}) • ${property.Weather.FetchedAtLocal} UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset} 
    • Body: Paste your HTML + CSS email UI Code inside the Body section. 
    • Body Mime Type: Text/HTML

HTML/CSS Code
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
<meta charset="UTF-8">
<title>Weather Update – ${property.Weather.Location}</title>
<meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"/>
<style>
  body { font-family: -apple-system, Segoe UI, Roboto, Arial, sans-serif; margin:0; padding:0; color:#1f2937; }
  .container { max-width: 640px; margin: 24px auto; background: #ffffff; border-radius: 16px; overflow: hidden; box-shadow: 0 10px 25px rgba(17,24,39,.08); }
  .header { background: #2563eb; color:#fff; padding: 20px 24px; }
  .title { margin:0; font-size: 20px; font-weight: 700; letter-spacing: .2px; }
  .subtitle { margin:6px 0 0; font-size: 13px; opacity: .95; }
  .content { padding: 20px 24px; }
  .tempCard { display:flex; align-items:center; justify-content:space-between; background:#f8fafc; border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:12px; padding:16px; margin:8px; }
  .tempMain { font-size: 38px; font-weight: 800; color:#111827; }
  .tempFeel { font-size: 14px; color:#6b7280; }
  .badge { display:inline-block; padding:6px 12px; font-size:12px; border-radius:999px; background:${property.Weather.CategoryColor}; color:#fff; }
  .row { display:flex; gap:20px; flex-wrap: wrap; }
  .card { flex:1 1 180px; min-width:180px; background:#ffffff; border:1px solid #e5e7eb; border-radius:12px; padding:14px; margin:8px;}
  .card h4 { margin:0 0 8px; font-size:13px; color:#374151; text-transform:uppercase; letter-spacing:.5px; }
  .value { font-size:16px; color:#111827; font-weight:600; }
  .dim { color:#6b7280; font-size:12px; }
  .footer { padding: 16px 24px; background:#f8fafc; border-top:1px solid #e5e7eb; color:#6b7280; font-size:12px; }
</style>
</head>

<body>
  <div class="container">
    
    <div class="header">
      <h1 class="title">Weather in ${property.Weather.Location}</h1>
      <p class="subtitle">
        As of ${property.Weather.FetchedAtLocal} (UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset})
      </p>
    </div>

    <div class="content">
      
      <div class="tempCard">
        <div>
          <div class="tempMain">${property.Weather.TempC}°C</div>
          <div class="tempFeel">
            Feels like ${property.Weather.FeelsLikeC}°C • 
            ${property.Weather.Condition} – ${property.Weather.Description}
          </div>
        </div>
        <div>
          <span class="badge">${property.Weather.Category}</span>
        </div>
      </div>

      <div class="row">

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Humidity</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.Humidity}%</div>
          <div class="dim">Comfort varies with temp & wind</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Pressure</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.Pressure} hPa</div>
          <div class="dim">Sea-level equivalent</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Visibility</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.VisibilityKm} km</div>
          <div class="dim">Reported by station</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Wind</h4>
          <div class="value">
            ${property.Weather.WindSpeedMS} m/s • 
            ${property.Weather.WindDir} (${property.Weather.WindDeg}°)
          </div>
          <div class="dim">Direction & speed</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Cloud Cover</h4>
          <div class="value">${property.Weather.CloudsPct}%</div>
          <div class="dim">Fraction of sky covered</div>
        </div>

        <div class="card">
          <h4>Sunrise / Sunset</h4>
          <div class="value">
            ${property.Weather.SunriseLocal} • ${property.Weather.SunsetLocal}
          </div>
          <div class="dim">Local times</div>
        </div>

      </div>
    </div>

    <div class="footer">
      Data source: OpenWeather • 
      Local date: ${property.Weather.LocalDate} • 
      Timezone: UTC${property.Weather.TimezoneOffset}
    </div>

  </div>
</body>
</html>

DEPLOY & TESTING

STEPS 1: Deploy the iFlow

  • Click Deploy in CPI. 
  • Wait until the deployment status becomes “Started”. 
  • Go to Monitor → Manage Integration Content. 
  • Confirm the iFlow is Started (green status).

STEPS 2: Test the iFlow 

  • Set the schedule to a short interval (e.g., every 5 minutes) during testing. 
  • Once the scheduled time arrives, CPI will trigger the iFlow automatically.
  • Go to Monitor → Message Processing and verify: 
  • Status: Completed 
  • Check your email inbox for the weather alert.

CONCLUSION

With just a few essential steps—Timer, API call via Request‑Reply, Groovy-based temperature evaluation, and the Mail adapter—you can deliver clear and modern HTML weather alerts exactly when they are needed. This approach can also be adapted for other rule‑based notification scenarios such as air‑quality updates, inventory thresholds, or SLA exception alerts.

If you’d like to extend this further, you can experiment with features like multi‑city summaries, analytical dashboards, or sending notifications to channels like Microsoft Teams or Slack.

If you have any questions or would like to share your feedback, feel free to drop a comment!

entry.title: Architecting Electronic Bill of Lading on SAP BTP - Rethinking Paper-Based Trade (Part 1 of 3)
entry.content:
Introduction

After participating in the SAP Multidimensional Architect Program at the SAP Academy  in California during Fall 2025, I was particularly inspired by a session titled “From Blueprint to Real Architecture,” which showed how to design end-to-end solutions, one of the cases presented, was international trade scenario, area of my personal interest over the last few years. That experience motivated me to write this Proof of Concept.

This article focuses on the scenario of Ocean Carriers transitioning from paper-based processes to a fully digital platform, aiming to reduce CO₂ emissions while ensuring security and transparency among stakeholders. The solution implements the Electronic Bill of Lading using IMDA's  TradeTrust , an open-source framework that enables the creation of digital trade documents represented as NFT(ERC-721 tokens) on a blockchain network.

This PoC demonstrates how this capability can be deployed on SAP Business Technology Platform, aligned with Digital Container Shipping Association (DCSA)  standards.

 

Article Summary

This three-part series demonstrates how international trade can move from paper-based operations to secure and automate processes while helping organizations achieve their sustainability goals

  • Part 1 – Case Study: Introduces the shift in global trade from paper-based documents to Electronic Bills of Lading.
  • Part 2 – Blueprint:  Describes the key building blocks of the solution, including: event-driven architecture, SAP BTP, Automation, TradeTrust Framework, UI, and the architecture diagram.
  • Part 3 – End-to-End Scenario: Shows the lifecycle of the Electronic Bill of Lading through a real-world scenario.

 

1. Case Study

The global shipping industry, responsible for approximately 90% of world trade, faces pressure to reduce its environmental footprint. Traditional paper-based Bills of Lading, critical for international trade, contribute to deforestation, water pollution, and greenhouse gas emissions through their production, transportation, and disposal.

2. Business Context

Paper Bills of Lading(BL) plays a crucial role in international trade, serve as Receipt, Contract and Document of title. This document is been the core of international trade for centuries.

  • Receipt: The BL, which is issued by the carrier, serves as proof that the cargo has been loaded onto the vessel, documenting the quantity and condition of the goods at the time of shipment.
  • Contract: It acts as a contract of carriage between the shipper, carrier and consignee, defining the agreed terms and conditions of transportation.
  • Document of title: It acts as a document of title, representing ownership of the goods while they are in transit.
